{{draft categories| [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 7]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Ancient swords of Japan]] [[Category:Individual Japanese swords]] [[Category:Mythological swords]] [[Category:Regalia]] [[Category:Japanese mythology]] [[Category:Kusanagi no Tsurugi|*]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Kusanagi no Tsurugi]] [[en:Kusanagi no Tsurugi]]

{{Redirect|Kusanagi|3=Kusanagi (disambiguation)}} {{short description|Legendary sword of the Imperial Regalia of Japan}} {{italic title|reason=[[Japanese words and phrases]]}} {{Multiple issues| {{refimprove|date=November 2011}} {{more footnotes|date=November 2011}} }} {{jmyth navbox tall}} {{nihongo||{{linktext|草|薙|の|剣}}|'''Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi'''}} is a legendary Japanese [[sword]] and one of three [[Imperial Regalia of Japan]]. It was originally called {{nihongo3|"Heavenly Sword of Gathering Clouds"|{{linktext|天|叢|雲|剣}}|'''Ame-no-Murakumo-no-Tsurugi'''}}, but its name was later changed to the more popular {{transl|ja|Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi}} ("Grass-Cutting Sword"). In folklore, the sword represents the [[virtue]] of [[Courage|valor]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last=Perez|first=Louis G.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=RHXG0JV9zEkC&pg=PA191|title=Japan at War: An Encyclopedia|date=2013|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-59884-741-3|pages=191–192|language=en}}</ref>
==Legends== [[File:YamataNoOrochi.jpg|thumb|Susanoo slays the eight-headed beast Yamata-no-Orochi]] [[File:Yamato-Takeru-with-Sword-Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi-by-Ogata-Gekko.png|thumb|Prince [[Yamato Takeru]] and his sword Kusanagi no Tsurugi]] The history of the {{transl|ja|Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi}} extends into legend. According to {{transl|ja|[[Kojiki]]}}, the god [[Susanoo]] encountered a grieving family of {{transl|ja|kunitsukami}} ("gods of the land") headed by {{nihongo|Ashinazuchi|足名椎}} in [[Izumo Province]]. When Susanoo inquired of Ashinazuchi, he told him that his family was being terrorized by the fearsome [[Yamata no Orochi]], an eight-headed serpent of [[Koshi Province (Japan)|Koshi]], who had consumed seven of the family's eight daughters, and that the creature was coming for his final daughter, {{nihongo|[[Kushinada-hime]]|奇稲田姫}}. Susanoo investigated the creature, and after an abortive encounter he returned with a plan to defeat it. In return, he asked for Kushinada-hime's hand in marriage, which was agreed. Transforming her temporarily into a comb (one interpreter reads this section as "using a comb he turns into [masquerades as] Kushinada-hime") to have her company during battle, he detailed his plan into steps.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&pg=PA110|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|pages=110|language=en}}</ref>
He instructed that eight vats of {{transl|ja|[[sake]]}} (rice wine) be prepared and put on individual platforms positioned behind a fence with eight gates. The monster took the bait and put one of its heads through each gate. With this distraction, Susanoo attacked and slew the beast (with his sword {{transl|ja|[[Worochi no Ara-masa]]}}),<ref>{{cite book |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |translator-link=William George Aston |translator-first=William George |translator-last=Aston |at=Book I, part 1, page 56 |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |date=2005 |isbn=9780804836746}}</ref> chopping off each head and then proceeded to do the same to the tails.<ref name=":1" /> In the fourth tail, he discovered a great sword inside the body of the serpent which he called {{transl|ja|Ame-no-Murakumo-no-Tsurugi}}. He presented the sword to the goddess [[Amaterasu]] to settle an old grievance.<ref name=":0" /> The [[Nihon Shoki]] adds more to the story. It says Susanoo had [[Ame-no-Fuyukinu]] deliver the sword.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web |date=2023-11-14 |title=Amenofuyukinu, Amenofukine {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9436 |access-date=2023-11-14 |archive-date=2023-11-14 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231114035349/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9436 |url-status=bot: unknown }}</ref> A rite at [[Hinomisaki Shrine]] honors this delivery to this day.<ref name=":2" />
[[File:Atsuta Shrine.jpg|thumb|[[Atsuta Shrine]] in [[Nagoya]] dates back to 100 CE during the reign of [[Emperor Keikō]] and houses the {{transl|ja|Kusanagi}} sword.]]
Generations later, during the reign of the 12th Emperor [[Emperor Keikō|Keikō]], {{transl|ja|Ame-no-Murakumo-no-Tsurugi}} was given to the great warrior, [[Yamato Takeru]], as part of a pair of gifts given by his aunt, [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]], the Shrine Maiden of [[Grand Shrine of Ise|Ise Shrine]], to protect her nephew in times of peril.<ref name=":0" />
These gifts came in handy when Yamato Takeru was lured onto an open grassland during a hunting expedition by a treacherous warlord. The lord had fiery arrows loosed to ignite the grass and trap Yamato Takeru in the field so that he would burn to death. He also killed the warrior's horse to prevent his escape. Desperately, Yamato Takeru used the {{transl|ja|Ame-no-Murakumo-no-Tsurugi}} to cut back the grass and remove fuel from the fire, but in doing so, he discovered that the sword enabled him to control the wind and cause it to move in the direction of his swing. Taking advantage of this magic, Yamato Takeru used his other gift, fire strikers, to enlarge the fire in the direction of the lord and his men, and he used the winds controlled by the sword to sweep the blaze toward them. In triumph, Yamato Takeru renamed the sword {{transl|ja|Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi}} ("Grass-Cutting Sword") to commemorate his narrow escape and victory. Eventually, Yamato Takeru married and later fell in battle against a monster, after ignoring his wife's advice to take the sword with him.<ref name=":0" />
==Folklore== Although the sword is mentioned in the {{transl|ja|[[Kojiki]]}}, this book is a collection of Japanese myths and is not considered a historical document. The first reliable historical mention of the sword is in the {{transl|ja|[[Nihon Shoki]]}}.<ref name="Nihongi">{{cite book |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |translator-link=William George Aston |translator-first=William George |translator-last=Aston |at=Book I, part 1, page 53 |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |date=2005 |isbn=9780804836746}}</ref> Although the {{transl|ja|Nihon Shoki}} also contains mythological stories that are not considered reliable history, it records some events that were contemporary or nearly contemporary to its writing, and these sections of the book are considered historical. In the {{transl|ja|Nihon Shoki}}, the Kusanagi was removed from the Imperial palace in 688, and moved to [[Atsuta Shrine]] after the sword was blamed for causing [[Emperor Tenmu]] to fall ill. Along with the [[Magatama#Yasakani no Magatama|jewel]] ({{transl|ja|Yasakani no Magatama}}) and the [[Yata no Kagami|mirror]] ({{transl|ja|Yata no Kagami}}), it is one of the three [[Imperial Regalia of Japan]], the sword representing the virtue of valor.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&pg=PA115|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|pages=115|language=en}}</ref>
[[File:三種の神器 1200x1200.png|thumb|Artist's impressions of the (unseen) Imperial Regalia of Japan]]
{{transl|ja|Kusanagi}} is allegedly kept at Atsuta Shrine but is not available for public display.<ref name=":0" /> During the [[Edo period]], while performing various repairs and upkeep at Atsuta Shrine, including replacement of the outer wooden box housing the sword, the Shinto priest Matsuoka Masanao claimed to have been one of several priests to have seen the sword. Per his account, "a stone box was inside a wooden box of length {{convert|150|cm|abbr=on}}, with red clay stuffed into the gap between them. Inside the stone box was a hollowed log of a camphor tree, acting as another box, with an interior lined with gold. Above that was placed a sword. Red clay was also stuffed between the stone box and the camphor tree box. The sword was about {{convert|82|cm|abbr=on}} long. Its blade resembled a [[Sweet flag|calamus]] leaf. The middle of the sword had a thickness from the grip about {{convert|18|cm|abbr=on}} with an appearance like a fish spine. The sword was fashioned in a white metallic color, and well maintained." After witnessing the sword, the grand priest was banished and the other priests, except for Matsuoka, died from strange diseases. The above account therefore comes from the only survivor, Matsuoka.<ref>First appears in {{Nihongo|Endorsement of Gyokusensyu|玉籤集裏書|Gyokusenshū uragaki}} from around 1725, written by [[:ja:玉木正英|Tamaki Masahide]], a Shinto priest of [[Umenomiya Shrine]]. This text is then mentioned in the {{Nihongo|Investigation of Imperial Regalia|神器考證|Jingi Kōshō}}, published 1898, written by [[:ja:栗田寛|Kurita Hiroshi]]. Kokugakuin. http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/815487</ref><!--Another record claims that this priest died from the curse and the power of the sword, but this is most likely a story that was spread to emphasize its power.{{Citation needed|date=December 2008}}-->
In ''[[The Tale of the Heike]]'', a collection of oral stories transcribed in 1371, the sword is lost at sea after the defeat of the [[Taira clan|Heike]] in the [[Battle of Dan-no-ura]], a naval battle that ended in the defeat of the Heike clan forces and the child [[Emperor Antoku]] at the hands of [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]]. In the tale, upon hearing of the Navy's defeat, the Emperor's grandmother, [[Taira no Tokiko]], led the Emperor and his entourage to commit suicide by drowning in the waters of the strait, taking with her two of the three Imperial Regalia: the sacred jewel and the sword Kusanagi.<ref name="Tales 2006">{{cite book |title=The Tales of the Heike |url=https://archive.org/details/talesheiketransl00wats |url-access=limited |page=[https://archive.org/details/talesheiketransl00wats/page/n154 142] |publisher=Columbia University Press |date=2006 |isbn=9780231138024 |translator-last1=Watson |translator-first1=Burton}}</ref> The sacred mirror was recovered in extremis when one of the ladies-in-waiting was about to jump with it into the sea.<ref name="Tales 1988">{{cite book |title=The Tale of the Heike |translator-last=McCullough |translator-first=Helen Craig |publisher=Stanford University Press |date=1988 |isbn=9780804714181}}</ref> Although the sacred jewel is said to have been found in its casket floating on the waves, {{transl|ja|Kusanagi}} was lost forever. Although written about historical events, ''[[The Tale of the Heike]]'' is a collection of epic poetry passed down orally and written down nearly 200 years after the actual events, so it has questionable reliability as a historical document.
Another story holds that the sword was reportedly stolen again in the sixth century by a monk from [[Silla]].<ref name="Tales 1988" /> However, his ship allegedly sank at sea, allowing the sword to wash ashore at Ise, where it was recovered by Shinto priests.
==Current status== Because no one is allowed to see the sword due to its divinity and Shinto tradition, the exact shape and condition of the sword has not been confirmed. The most recent appearance of the sword was in 2019 when Emperor [[Naruhito]] [[2019 Japanese imperial transition|ascended the throne]]; the sword (as well as the jewel [[Yasakani no Magatama]], the Emperor's [[Privy Seal of Japan|privy seal]] and the [[State Seal of Japan|State Seal]]) were shrouded in packages.
Replicas of the sword were made as early as the 9th century, and the original is entrusted to [[Atsuta Shrine]] in [[Nagoya]].<ref name="encyclopedia-imp">{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Encyclopedia of Japan | title = Kurayoshi Plain | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-04-12 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo}}</ref> According to Shinsuke Takenaka of the Institute of Moralogy, a 12th-century replica preserved in the Imperial palace is the one used in coronation ceremonies,<ref name="bbctres">{{cite web |title=Akihito and Japan's Imperial Treasures that make a man an emperor |website=BBC |author=Anna Jones |date=27 April 2019 |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/world-asia-47931671 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220324032626/https://www.bbc.com/news/world-asia-47931671 |archive-date=March 24, 2022}}</ref> probably due to the fragility of the original sword.
==Other emperors' swords== The {{transl|ja|Kusanagi}} sword is always hidden because of its divinity, and it is put in a box and put up by the chamberlain at the time of the [[Enthronement of the Japanese emperor|enthronement ceremony]]. However, the Japanese sword held up by the emperor's chamberlain, which can be seen at various imperial ceremonies, is always close to the emperor as an amulet, and is called {{nihongo3|meaning 'sword of the throne in the daytime'|昼御座御剣|Hi no Omashi no Gyoken}}. {{transl|ja|Hi no Omashi no Gyoken}} has changed over time; at present, two {{transl|ja|[[tachi]]}} made by swordsmiths Nagamitsu and Yukihira in the [[Kamakura period]] play the role.<ref name="hachi191108">[https://twitter.com/EeoduLzbYVjTprk/status/1192601246122643456 儀式で天皇陛下のお側に登場する太刀はもちろん神器ではなく、平安以来の天皇の守り刀「昼御座御剣（ひのおましのごけん）」]. Tadamoto Hachijō. November 8, 2019</ref> Apart from these swords, the Imperial Family owns many swords, which are managed by the Imperial Household Agency. For example, one of the {{transl|ja|[[Tenka-Goken]]}}, {{transl|ja|Onimaru}} is owned by the Imperial Family.
==The sword of the Crown Prince of Japan== The Japanese crown prince has inherited two {{transl|ja|[[tachi]]}}, {{transl|ja|Tsubokiri no Gyoken}} or {{nihongo3|meaning "sword that cut a pot"|[[:ja:壺切御剣|壺切御剣]]|Tsubokiri no Mitsurugi}}, and {{nihongo3|meaning "sword made by Yukihira"|行平御剣|Yukihira Gyoken}}. While the {{transl|ja|Kusanagi}} sword is forbidden to be seen because of its divinity and is always kept in a box, the Crown Prince's sword is worn by the Crown Prince with the traditional costume {{transl|ja|[[sokutai]]}} at an official ceremony of the Imperial Household.<ref name="mai201107">{{Cite web|url=https://mainichi.jp/articles/20201107/k00/00m/040/036000c|title=秋篠宮さまに授けられる皇太子の守り刀 ベールに包まれた「壺切御剣」とは?|date=November 7, 2020|access-date=November 7, 2020|publisher=[[Mainichi Shimbun]]|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201107040543/https://mainichi.jp/articles/20201107/k00/00m/040/036000c|archive-date=2020-11-07}}</ref>
The {{transl|ja|Tsubokiri}} sword is the most important sword owned by the Crown Prince, given by the Emperor as proof of the official Crown Prince after the ceremony of his inauguration. Its origin is that it was given by [[Emperor Uda]] when [[Emperor Daigo]] became Crown Prince in 893, and the present, the {{transl|ja|Tsubokiri}} sword is the second generation, made in the late [[Heian period]]. The {{transl|ja|Yukihira}} sword is a {{transl|ja|tachi}} made by Yukihira, a swordsmith in the [[Kamakura period]], and the Crown Prince inherits it from the Emperor before his inauguration ceremony and wears it in various Imperial events except for the {{transl|ja|[[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niiname-sai]]}} festival.<ref name="mai201107"/> This Yukihira sword is different from the Emperor's {{transl|ja|Hi no Omashi no Gyoken}}.<ref name="hachi191108"/>
==See also== * [[Japanese sword]] * [[Ame-no-ohabari]]
==References== {{reflist|30em}} *{{Cite web |url=http://www.freidok.uni-freiburg.de/volltexte/4635/pdf/Naumann_The_kusanagi_sword.pdf |title=The kusanagi sword |first=Nelly |last=Naumann |date=1992 |author-link=Nelly Naumann }} In ''Nenrin-Jahresringe: Festgabe für Hans A. Dettmer.'' Ed. Klaus Müller. Wiesbaden: Harrassowitz, 1992. [158]–170.

revision.text:
{{Redirect|Kusanagi|3=Kusanagi (disambiguation)}} {{short description|Legendary sword of the Imperial Regalia of Japan}} {{italic title|reason=[[Japanese words and phrases]]}} {{Multiple issues| {{refimprove|date=November 2011}} {{more footnotes|date=November 2011}} }} {{jmyth navbox tall}} {{nihongo||{{linktext|草|薙|の|剣}}|'''Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi'''}} is a legendary Japanese [[sword]] and one of three [[Imperial Regalia of Japan]]. It was originally called {{nihongo3|"Heavenly Sword of Gathering Clouds"|{{linktext|天|叢|雲|剣}}|'''Ame-no-Murakumo-no-Tsurugi'''}}, but its name was later changed to the more popular {{transl|ja|Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi}} ("Grass-Cutting Sword"). In folklore, the sword represents the [[virtue]] of [[Courage|valor]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last=Perez|first=Louis G.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=RHXG0JV9zEkC&pg=PA191|title=Japan at War: An Encyclopedia|date=2013|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-59884-741-3|pages=191–192|language=en}}</ref>
==Legends== [[File:YamataNoOrochi.jpg|thumb|Susanoo slays the eight-headed beast Yamata-no-Orochi]] [[File:Yamato-Takeru-with-Sword-Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi-by-Ogata-Gekko.png|thumb|Prince [[Yamato Takeru]] and his sword Kusanagi no Tsurugi]] The history of the {{transl|ja|Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi}} extends into legend. According to {{transl|ja|[[Kojiki]]}}, the god [[Susanoo]] encountered a grieving family of {{transl|ja|kunitsukami}} ("gods of the land") headed by {{nihongo|Ashinazuchi|足名椎}} in [[Izumo Province]]. When Susanoo inquired of Ashinazuchi, he told him that his family was being terrorized by the fearsome [[Yamata no Orochi]], an eight-headed serpent of [[Koshi Province (Japan)|Koshi]], who had consumed seven of the family's eight daughters, and that the creature was coming for his final daughter, {{nihongo|[[Kushinada-hime]]|奇稲田姫}}. Susanoo investigated the creature, and after an abortive encounter he returned with a plan to defeat it. In return, he asked for Kushinada-hime's hand in marriage, which was agreed. Transforming her temporarily into a comb (one interpreter reads this section as "using a comb he turns into [masquerades as] Kushinada-hime") to have her company during battle, he detailed his plan into steps.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&pg=PA110|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|pages=110|language=en}}</ref>
He instructed that eight vats of {{transl|ja|[[sake]]}} (rice wine) be prepared and put on individual platforms positioned behind a fence with eight gates. The monster took the bait and put one of its heads through each gate. With this distraction, Susanoo attacked and slew the beast (with his sword {{transl|ja|[[Worochi no Ara-masa]]}}),<ref>{{cite book |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |translator-link=William George Aston |translator-first=William George |translator-last=Aston |at=Book I, part 1, page 56 |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |date=2005 |isbn=9780804836746}}</ref> chopping off each head and then proceeded to do the same to the tails.<ref name=":1" /> In the fourth tail, he discovered a great sword inside the body of the serpent which he called {{transl|ja|Ame-no-Murakumo-no-Tsurugi}}. He presented the sword to the goddess [[Amaterasu]] to settle an old grievance.<ref name=":0" /> The [[Nihon Shoki]] adds more to the story. It says Susanoo had [[Ame-no-Fuyukinu]] deliver the sword.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web |date=2023-11-14 |title=Amenofuyukinu, Amenofukine {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9436 |access-date=2023-11-14 |archive-date=2023-11-14 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231114035349/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9436 |url-status=bot: unknown }}</ref> A rite at [[Hinomisaki Shrine]] honors this delivery to this day.<ref name=":2" />
[[File:Atsuta Shrine.jpg|thumb|[[Atsuta Shrine]] in [[Nagoya]] dates back to 100 CE during the reign of [[Emperor Keikō]] and houses the {{transl|ja|Kusanagi}} sword.]]
Generations later, during the reign of the 12th Emperor [[Emperor Keikō|Keikō]], {{transl|ja|Ame-no-Murakumo-no-Tsurugi}} was given to the great warrior, [[Yamato Takeru]], as part of a pair of gifts given by his aunt, [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]], the Shrine Maiden of [[Grand Shrine of Ise|Ise Shrine]], to protect her nephew in times of peril.<ref name=":0" />
These gifts came in handy when Yamato Takeru was lured onto an open grassland during a hunting expedition by a treacherous warlord. The lord had fiery arrows loosed to ignite the grass and trap Yamato Takeru in the field so that he would burn to death. He also killed the warrior's horse to prevent his escape. Desperately, Yamato Takeru used the {{transl|ja|Ame-no-Murakumo-no-Tsurugi}} to cut back the grass and remove fuel from the fire, but in doing so, he discovered that the sword enabled him to control the wind and cause it to move in the direction of his swing. Taking advantage of this magic, Yamato Takeru used his other gift, fire strikers, to enlarge the fire in the direction of the lord and his men, and he used the winds controlled by the sword to sweep the blaze toward them. In triumph, Yamato Takeru renamed the sword {{transl|ja|Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi}} ("Grass-Cutting Sword") to commemorate his narrow escape and victory. Eventually, Yamato Takeru married and later fell in battle against a monster, after ignoring his wife's advice to take the sword with him.<ref name=":0" />
==Folklore== Although the sword is mentioned in the {{transl|ja|[[Kojiki]]}}, this book is a collection of Japanese myths and is not considered a historical document. The first reliable historical mention of the sword is in the {{transl|ja|[[Nihon Shoki]]}}.<ref name="Nihongi">{{cite book |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |translator-link=William George Aston |translator-first=William George |translator-last=Aston |at=Book I, part 1, page 53 |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |date=2005 |isbn=9780804836746}}</ref> Although the {{transl|ja|Nihon Shoki}} also contains mythological stories that are not considered reliable history, it records some events that were contemporary or nearly contemporary to its writing, and these sections of the book are considered historical. In the {{transl|ja|Nihon Shoki}}, the Kusanagi was removed from the Imperial palace in 688, and moved to [[Atsuta Shrine]] after the sword was blamed for causing [[Emperor Tenmu]] to fall ill. Along with the [[Magatama#Yasakani no Magatama|jewel]] ({{transl|ja|Yasakani no Magatama}}) and the [[Yata no Kagami|mirror]] ({{transl|ja|Yata no Kagami}}), it is one of the three [[Imperial Regalia of Japan]], the sword representing the virtue of valor.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&pg=PA115|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|pages=115|language=en}}</ref>
[[File:三種の神器 1200x1200.png|thumb|Artist's impressions of the (unseen) Imperial Regalia of Japan]]
{{transl|ja|Kusanagi}} is allegedly kept at Atsuta Shrine but is not available for public display.<ref name=":0" /> During the [[Edo period]], while performing various repairs and upkeep at Atsuta Shrine, including replacement of the outer wooden box housing the sword, the Shinto priest Matsuoka Masanao claimed to have been one of several priests to have seen the sword. Per his account, "a stone box was inside a wooden box of length {{convert|150|cm|abbr=on}}, with red clay stuffed into the gap between them. Inside the stone box was a hollowed log of a camphor tree, acting as another box, with an interior lined with gold. Above that was placed a sword. Red clay was also stuffed between the stone box and the camphor tree box. The sword was about {{convert|82|cm|abbr=on}} long. Its blade resembled a [[Sweet flag|calamus]] leaf. The middle of the sword had a thickness from the grip about {{convert|18|cm|abbr=on}} with an appearance like a fish spine. The sword was fashioned in a white metallic color, and well maintained." After witnessing the sword, the grand priest was banished and the other priests, except for Matsuoka, died from strange diseases. The above account therefore comes from the only survivor, Matsuoka.<ref>First appears in {{Nihongo|Endorsement of Gyokusensyu|玉籤集裏書|Gyokusenshū uragaki}} from around 1725, written by [[:ja:玉木正英|Tamaki Masahide]], a Shinto priest of [[Umenomiya Shrine]]. This text is then mentioned in the {{Nihongo|Investigation of Imperial Regalia|神器考證|Jingi Kōshō}}, published 1898, written by [[:ja:栗田寛|Kurita Hiroshi]]. Kokugakuin. http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/815487</ref>
In ''[[The Tale of the Heike]]'', a collection of oral stories transcribed in 1371, the sword is lost at sea after the defeat of the [[Taira clan|Heike]] in the [[Battle of Dan-no-ura]], a naval battle that ended in the defeat of the Heike clan forces and the child [[Emperor Antoku]] at the hands of [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]]. In the tale, upon hearing of the Navy's defeat, the Emperor's grandmother, [[Taira no Tokiko]], led the Emperor and his entourage to commit suicide by drowning in the waters of the strait, taking with her two of the three Imperial Regalia: the sacred jewel and the sword Kusanagi.<ref name="Tales 2006">{{cite book |title=The Tales of the Heike |url=https://archive.org/details/talesheiketransl00wats |url-access=limited |page=[https://archive.org/details/talesheiketransl00wats/page/n154 142] |publisher=Columbia University Press |date=2006 |isbn=9780231138024 |translator-last1=Watson |translator-first1=Burton}}</ref> The sacred mirror was recovered in extremis when one of the ladies-in-waiting was about to jump with it into the sea.<ref name="Tales 1988">{{cite book |title=The Tale of the Heike |translator-last=McCullough |translator-first=Helen Craig |publisher=Stanford University Press |date=1988 |isbn=9780804714181}}</ref> Although the sacred jewel is said to have been found in its casket floating on the waves, {{transl|ja|Kusanagi}} was lost forever. Although written about historical events, ''[[The Tale of the Heike]]'' is a collection of epic poetry passed down orally and written down nearly 200 years after the actual events, so it has questionable reliability as a historical document.
Another story holds that the sword was reportedly stolen again in the sixth century by a monk from [[Silla]].<ref name="Tales 1988" /> However, his ship allegedly sank at sea, allowing the sword to wash ashore at Ise, where it was recovered by Shinto priests.
==Current status== Because no one is allowed to see the sword due to its divinity and Shinto tradition, the exact shape and condition of the sword has not been confirmed. The most recent appearance of the sword was in 2019 when Emperor [[Naruhito]] [[2019 Japanese imperial transition|ascended the throne]]; the sword (as well as the jewel [[Yasakani no Magatama]], the Emperor's [[Privy Seal of Japan|privy seal]] and the [[State Seal of Japan|State Seal]]) were shrouded in packages.
Replicas of the sword were made as early as the 9th century, and the original is entrusted to [[Atsuta Shrine]] in [[Nagoya]].<ref name="encyclopedia-imp">{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Encyclopedia of Japan | title = Kurayoshi Plain | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-04-12 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo}}</ref> According to Shinsuke Takenaka of the Institute of Moralogy, a 12th-century replica preserved in the Imperial palace is the one used in coronation ceremonies,<ref name="bbctres">{{cite web |title=Akihito and Japan's Imperial Treasures that make a man an emperor |website=BBC |author=Anna Jones |date=27 April 2019 |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/world-asia-47931671 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220324032626/https://www.bbc.com/news/world-asia-47931671 |archive-date=March 24, 2022}}</ref> probably due to the fragility of the original sword.
==Other emperors' swords== The {{transl|ja|Kusanagi}} sword is always hidden because of its divinity, and it is put in a box and put up by the chamberlain at the time of the [[Enthronement of the Japanese emperor|enthronement ceremony]]. However, the Japanese sword held up by the emperor's chamberlain, which can be seen at various imperial ceremonies, is always close to the emperor as an amulet, and is called {{nihongo3|meaning 'sword of the throne in the daytime'|昼御座御剣|Hi no Omashi no Gyoken}}. {{transl|ja|Hi no Omashi no Gyoken}} has changed over time; at present, two {{transl|ja|[[tachi]]}} made by swordsmiths Nagamitsu and Yukihira in the [[Kamakura period]] play the role.<ref name="hachi191108">[https://twitter.com/EeoduLzbYVjTprk/status/1192601246122643456 儀式で天皇陛下のお側に登場する太刀はもちろん神器ではなく、平安以来の天皇の守り刀「昼御座御剣（ひのおましのごけん）」]. Tadamoto Hachijō. November 8, 2019</ref> Apart from these swords, the Imperial Family owns many swords, which are managed by the Imperial Household Agency. For example, one of the {{transl|ja|[[Tenka-Goken]]}}, {{transl|ja|Onimaru}} is owned by the Imperial Family.
==The sword of the Crown Prince of Japan== The Japanese crown prince has inherited two {{transl|ja|[[tachi]]}}, {{transl|ja|Tsubokiri no Gyoken}} or {{nihongo3|meaning "sword that cut a pot"|[[:ja:壺切御剣|壺切御剣]]|Tsubokiri no Mitsurugi}}, and {{nihongo3|meaning "sword made by Yukihira"|行平御剣|Yukihira Gyoken}}. While the {{transl|ja|Kusanagi}} sword is forbidden to be seen because of its divinity and is always kept in a box, the Crown Prince's sword is worn by the Crown Prince with the traditional costume {{transl|ja|[[sokutai]]}} at an official ceremony of the Imperial Household.<ref name="mai201107">{{Cite web|url=https://mainichi.jp/articles/20201107/k00/00m/040/036000c|title=秋篠宮さまに授けられる皇太子の守り刀 ベールに包まれた「壺切御剣」とは?|date=November 7, 2020|access-date=November 7, 2020|publisher=[[Mainichi Shimbun]]|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201107040543/https://mainichi.jp/articles/20201107/k00/00m/040/036000c|archive-date=2020-11-07}}</ref>
The {{transl|ja|Tsubokiri}} sword is the most important sword owned by the Crown Prince, given by the Emperor as proof of the official Crown Prince after the ceremony of his inauguration. Its origin is that it was given by [[Emperor Uda]] when [[Emperor Daigo]] became Crown Prince in 893, and the present, the {{transl|ja|Tsubokiri}} sword is the second generation, made in the late [[Heian period]]. The {{transl|ja|Yukihira}} sword is a {{transl|ja|tachi}} made by Yukihira, a swordsmith in the [[Kamakura period]], and the Crown Prince inherits it from the Emperor before his inauguration ceremony and wears it in various Imperial events except for the {{transl|ja|[[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niiname-sai]]}} festival.<ref name="mai201107"/> This Yukihira sword is different from the Emperor's {{transl|ja|Hi no Omashi no Gyoken}}.<ref name="hachi191108"/>
==See also== * [[Japanese sword]] * [[Ame-no-ohabari]]
==References== {{reflist|30em}} *{{Cite web |url=http://www.freidok.uni-freiburg.de/volltexte/4635/pdf/Naumann_The_kusanagi_sword.pdf |title=The kusanagi sword |first=Nelly |last=Naumann |date=1992 |author-link=Nelly Naumann }} In ''Nenrin-Jahresringe: Festgabe für Hans A. Dettmer.'' Ed. Klaus Müller. Wiesbaden: Harrassowitz, 1992. [158]–170.

revision.text:
{{Redirect|Kusanagi|3=Kusanagi (disambiguation)}} {{short description|Legendary sword of the Imperial Regalia of Japan}} {{italic title|reason=[[Japanese words and phrases]]}} {{Multiple issues| {{refimprove|date=November 2011}} {{more footnotes|date=November 2011}} }} {{jmyth navbox tall}} {{nihongo||{{linktext|草|薙|の|剣}}|'''Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi'''}} is a legendary Japanese [[sword]] and one of three [[Imperial Regalia of Japan]]. It was originally called {{nihongo3|"Heavenly Sword of Gathering Clouds"|{{linktext|天|叢|雲|剣}}|'''Ame-no-Murakumo-no-Tsurugi'''}}, but its name was later changed to the more popular {{transl|ja|Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi}} ("Grass-Cutting Sword"). In folklore, the sword represents the [[virtue]] of [[Courage|valor]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last=Perez|first=Louis G.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=RHXG0JV9zEkC&pg=PA191|title=Japan at War: An Encyclopedia|date=2013|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-59884-741-3|pages=191–192|language=en}}</ref>
==Legends== [[File:YamataNoOrochi.jpg|thumb|Susanoo slays the eight-headed beast Yamata-no-Orochi]] [[File:Yamato-Takeru-with-Sword-Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi-by-Ogata-Gekko.png|thumb|Prince [[Yamato Takeru]] and his sword Kusanagi no Tsurugi]] The history of the {{transl|ja|Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi}} extends into legend. According to {{transl|ja|[[Kojiki]]}}, the god [[Susanoo]] encountered a grieving family of {{transl|ja|kunitsukami}} ("gods of the land") headed by {{nihongo|Ashinazuchi|足名椎}} in [[Izumo Province]]. When Susanoo inquired of Ashinazuchi, he told him that his family was being terrorized by the fearsome [[Yamata no Orochi]], an eight-headed serpent of [[Koshi Province (Japan)|Koshi]], who had consumed seven of the family's eight daughters, and that the creature was coming for his final daughter, {{nihongo|[[Kushinada-hime]]|奇稲田姫}}. Susanoo investigated the creature, and after an abortive encounter he returned with a plan to defeat it. In return, he asked for Kushinada-hime's hand in marriage, which was agreed. Transforming her temporarily into a comb (one interpreter reads this section as "using a comb he turns into [masquerades as] Kushinada-hime") to have her company during battle, he detailed his plan into steps.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&pg=PA110|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|pages=110|language=en}}</ref>
He instructed that eight vats of {{transl|ja|[[sake]]}} (rice wine) be prepared and put on individual platforms positioned behind a fence with eight gates. The monster took the bait and put one of its heads through each gate. With this distraction, Susanoo attacked and slew the beast (with his sword {{transl|ja|[[Worochi no Ara-masa]]}}),<ref>{{cite book |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |translator-link=William George Aston |translator-first=William George |translator-last=Aston |at=Book I, part 1, page 56 |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |date=2005 |isbn=9780804836746}}</ref> chopping off each head and then proceeded to do the same to the tails.<ref name=":1" /> In the fourth tail, he discovered a great sword inside the body of the serpent which he called {{transl|ja|Ame-no-Murakumo-no-Tsurugi}}. He presented the sword to the goddess [[Amaterasu]] to settle an old grievance.<ref name=":0" /> The [[Nihon Shoki]] adds more to the story. It says Susanoo had [[Ame-no-Fuyukinu]] deliver the sword.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web |date=2023-11-14 |title=Amenofuyukinu, Amenofukine {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9436 |access-date=2023-11-14 |archive-date=2023-11-14 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231114035349/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9436 |url-status=bot: unknown }}</ref> A rite at [[Hinomisaki Shrine]] honors this delivery to this day.<ref name=":2" />
[[File:Atsuta Shrine.jpg|thumb|[[Atsuta Shrine]] in [[Nagoya]] dates back to 100 CE during the reign of [[Emperor Keikō]] and houses the {{transl|ja|Kusanagi}} sword.]]
Generations later, during the reign of the 12th Emperor [[Emperor Keikō|Keikō]], {{transl|ja|Ame-no-Murakumo-no-Tsurugi}} was given to the great warrior, [[Yamato Takeru]], as part of a pair of gifts given by his aunt, [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]], the Shrine Maiden of [[Grand Shrine of Ise|Ise Shrine]], to protect her nephew in times of peril.<ref name=":0" />
These gifts came in handy when Yamato Takeru was lured onto an open grassland during a hunting expedition by a treacherous warlord. The lord had fiery arrows loosed to ignite the grass and trap Yamato Takeru in the field so that he would burn to death. He also killed the warrior's horse to prevent his escape. Desperately, Yamato Takeru used the {{transl|ja|Ame-no-Murakumo-no-Tsurugi}} to cut back the grass and remove fuel from the fire, but in doing so, he discovered that the sword enabled him to control the wind and cause it to move in the direction of his swing. Taking advantage of this magic, Yamato Takeru used his other gift, fire strikers, to enlarge the fire in the direction of the lord and his men, and he used the winds controlled by the sword to sweep the blaze toward them. In triumph, Yamato Takeru renamed the sword {{transl|ja|Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi}} ("Grass-Cutting Sword") to commemorate his narrow escape and victory. Eventually, Yamato Takeru married and later fell in battle against a monster, after ignoring his wife's advice to take the sword with him.<ref name=":0" />
==Folklore== Although the sword is mentioned in the {{transl|ja|[[Kojiki]]}}, this book is a collection of Japanese myths and is not considered a historical document. The first reliable historical mention of the sword is in the {{transl|ja|[[Nihon Shoki]]}}.<ref name="Nihongi">{{cite book |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |translator-link=William George Aston |translator-first=William George |translator-last=Aston |at=Book I, part 1, page 53 |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |date=2005 |isbn=9780804836746}}</ref> Although the {{transl|ja|Nihon Shoki}} also contains mythological stories that are not considered reliable history, it records some events that were contemporary or nearly contemporary to its writing, and these sections of the book are considered historical. In the {{transl|ja|Nihon Shoki}}, the Kusanagi was removed from the Imperial palace in 688, and moved to [[Atsuta Shrine]] after the sword was blamed for causing [[Emperor Tenmu]] to fall ill. Along with the [[Magatama#Yasakani no Magatama|jewel]] ({{transl|ja|Yasakani no Magatama}}) and the [[Yata no Kagami|mirror]] ({{transl|ja|Yata no Kagami}}), it is one of the three [[Imperial Regalia of Japan]], the sword representing the virtue of valor.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&pg=PA115|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|pages=115|language=en}}</ref>
[[File:三種の神器 1200x1200.png|thumb|Artist's impressions of the (unseen) Imperial Regalia of Japan]]
{{transl|ja|Kusanagi}} is allegedly kept at Atsuta Shrine but is not available for public display.<ref name=":0" /> During the [[Edo period]], while performing various repairs and upkeep at Atsuta Shrine, including replacement of the outer wooden box housing the sword, the Shinto priest Matsuoka Masanao claimed to have been one of several priests to have seen the sword. Per his account, "a stone box was inside a wooden box of length {{convert|150|cm|abbr=on}}, with red clay stuffed into the gap between them. Inside the stone box was a hollowed log of a camphor tree, acting as another box, with an interior lined with gold. Above that was placed a sword. Red clay was also stuffed between the stone box and the camphor tree box. The sword was about {{convert|82|cm|abbr=on}} long. Its blade resembled a [[Sweet flag|calamus]] leaf. The middle of the sword had a thickness from the grip about {{convert|18|cm|abbr=on}} with an appearance like a fish spine. The sword was fashioned in a white metallic color, and well maintained." After witnessing the sword, the grand priest was banished and the other priests, except for Matsuoka, died from strange diseases. The above account therefore comes from the only survivor, Matsuoka.<ref>First appears in {{Nihongo|Endorsement of Gyokusensyu|玉籤集裏書|Gyokusenshū uragaki}} from around 1725, written by [[:ja:玉木正英|Tamaki Masahide]], a Shinto priest of [[Umenomiya Shrine]]. This text is then mentioned in the {{Nihongo|Investigation of Imperial Regalia|神器考證|Jingi Kōshō}}, published 1898, written by [[:ja:栗田寛|Kurita Hiroshi]]. Kokugakuin. http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/815487</ref>
In ''[[The Tale of the Heike]]'', a collection of oral stories transcribed in 1371, the sword is lost at sea after the defeat of the [[Taira clan|Heike]] in the [[Battle of Dan-no-ura]], a naval battle that ended in the defeat of the Heike clan forces and the child [[Emperor Antoku]] at the hands of [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]]. In the tale, upon hearing of the Navy's defeat, the Emperor's grandmother, [[Taira no Tokiko]], led the Emperor and his entourage to commit suicide by drowning in the waters of the strait, taking with her two of the three Imperial Regalia: the sacred jewel and the sword Kusanagi.<ref name="Tales 2006">{{cite book |title=The Tales of the Heike |url=https://archive.org/details/talesheiketransl00wats |url-access=limited |page=[https://archive.org/details/talesheiketransl00wats/page/n154 142] |publisher=Columbia University Press |date=2006 |isbn=9780231138024 |translator-last1=Watson |translator-first1=Burton}}</ref> The sacred mirror was recovered in extremis when one of the ladies-in-waiting was about to jump with it into the sea.<ref name="Tales 1988">{{cite book |title=The Tale of the Heike |translator-last=McCullough |translator-first=Helen Craig |publisher=Stanford University Press |date=1988 |isbn=9780804714181}}</ref> Although the sacred jewel is said to have been found in its casket floating on the waves, {{transl|ja|Kusanagi}} was lost forever. Although written about historical events, ''[[The Tale of the Heike]]'' is a collection of epic poetry passed down orally and written down nearly 200 years after the actual events, so it has questionable reliability as a historical document.
Another story holds that the sword was reportedly stolen again in the sixth century by a monk from [[Silla]].<ref name="Tales 1988" /> However, his ship allegedly sank at sea, allowing the sword to wash ashore at Ise, where it was recovered by Shinto priests.
==Current status== Because no one is allowed to see the sword due to its divinity and Shinto tradition, the exact shape and condition of the sword has not been confirmed. The most recent appearance of the sword was in 2019 when Emperor [[Naruhito]] [[2019 Japanese imperial transition|ascended the throne]]; the sword (as well as the jewel [[Yasakani no Magatama]], the Emperor's [[Privy Seal of Japan|privy seal]] and the [[State Seal of Japan|State Seal]]) were shrouded in packages.
Replicas of the sword were made as early as the 9th century, and the original is entrusted to [[Atsuta Shrine]] in [[Nagoya]].<ref name="encyclopedia-imp">{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Encyclopedia of Japan | title = Kurayoshi Plain | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-04-12 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo}}</ref> According to Shinsuke Takenaka of the Institute of Moralogy, a 12th-century replica preserved in the Imperial palace is the one used in coronation ceremonies,<ref name="bbctres">{{cite web |title=Akihito and Japan's Imperial Treasures that make a man an emperor |website=BBC |author=Anna Jones |date=27 April 2019 |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/world-asia-47931671 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220324032626/https://www.bbc.com/news/world-asia-47931671 |archive-date=March 24, 2022}}</ref> probably due to the fragility of the original sword.
==Other emperors' swords== The {{transl|ja|Kusanagi}} sword is always hidden because of its divinity, and it is put in a box and put up by the chamberlain at the time of the [[Enthronement of the Japanese emperor|enthronement ceremony]]. However, the Japanese sword held up by the emperor's chamberlain, which can be seen at various imperial ceremonies, is always close to the emperor as an amulet, and is called {{nihongo3|meaning 'sword of the throne in the daytime'|昼御座御剣|Hi no Omashi no Gyoken}}. {{transl|ja|Hi no Omashi no Gyoken}} has changed over time; at present, two {{transl|ja|[[tachi]]}} made by swordsmiths Nagamitsu and Yukihira in the [[Kamakura period]] play the role.<ref name="hachi191108">[https://twitter.com/EeoduLzbYVjTprk/status/1192601246122643456 儀式で天皇陛下のお側に登場する太刀はもちろん神器ではなく、平安以来の天皇の守り刀「昼御座御剣（ひのおましのごけん）」]. Tadamoto Hachijō. November 8, 2019</ref> Apart from these swords, the Imperial Family owns many swords, which are managed by the Imperial Household Agency. For example, one of the {{transl|ja|[[Tenka-Goken]]}}, {{transl|ja|Onimaru}} is owned by the Imperial Family.
==The sword of the Crown Prince of Japan== The Japanese crown prince has inherited two {{transl|ja|[[tachi]]}}, {{transl|ja|Tsubokiri no Gyoken}} or {{nihongo3|meaning "sword that cut a pot"|[[:ja:壺切御剣|壺切御剣]]|Tsubokiri no Mitsurugi}}, and {{nihongo3|meaning "sword made by Yukihira"|行平御剣|Yukihira Gyoken}}. While the {{transl|ja|Kusanagi}} sword is forbidden to be seen because of its divinity and is always kept in a box, the Crown Prince's sword is worn by the Crown Prince with the traditional costume {{transl|ja|[[sokutai]]}} at an official ceremony of the Imperial Household.<ref name="mai201107">{{Cite web|url=https://mainichi.jp/articles/20201107/k00/00m/040/036000c|title=秋篠宮さまに授けられる皇太子の守り刀 ベールに包まれた「壺切御剣」とは?|date=November 7, 2020|access-date=November 7, 2020|publisher=[[Mainichi Shimbun]]|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201107040543/https://mainichi.jp/articles/20201107/k00/00m/040/036000c|archive-date=2020-11-07}}</ref>
The {{transl|ja|Tsubokiri}} sword is the most important sword owned by the Crown Prince, given by the Emperor as proof of the official Crown Prince after the ceremony of his inauguration. Its origin is that it was given by [[Emperor Uda]] when [[Emperor Daigo]] became Crown Prince in 893, and the present, the {{transl|ja|Tsubokiri}} sword is the second generation, made in the late [[Heian period]]. The {{transl|ja|Yukihira}} sword is a {{transl|ja|tachi}} made by Yukihira, a swordsmith in the [[Kamakura period]], and the Crown Prince inherits it from the Emperor before his inauguration ceremony and wears it in various Imperial events except for the {{transl|ja|[[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niiname-sai]]}} festival.<ref name="mai201107"/> This Yukihira sword is different from the Emperor's {{transl|ja|Hi no Omashi no Gyoken}}.<ref name="hachi191108"/>
==See also== * [[Japanese sword]] * [[Ame-no-ohabari]]
==References== {{reflist|30em}} *{{Cite web |url=http://www.freidok.uni-freiburg.de/volltexte/4635/pdf/Naumann_The_kusanagi_sword.pdf |title=The kusanagi sword |first=Nelly |last=Naumann |date=1992 |author-link=Nelly Naumann }} In ''Nenrin-Jahresringe: Festgabe für Hans A. Dettmer.'' Ed. Klaus Müller. Wiesbaden: Harrassowitz, 1992. [158]–170.

[[sn:Kusanagi no Tsurugi]] [[en:Kusanagi no Tsurugi]]
<!--Another record claims that this priest died from the curse and the power of the sword, but this is most likely a story that was spread to emphasize its power.{{Citation needed|date=December 2008}}--> [[Category:Ambox transclusions]]

revision.text:
{{Redirect|Kusanagi|3=Kusanagi (disambiguation)}} {{short description|Legendary sword of the Imperial Regalia of Japan}} {{italic title|reason=[[Japanese words and phrases]]}} {{Multiple issues| {{refimprove|date=November 2011}} {{more footnotes|date=November 2011}} }} {{jmyth navbox tall}} {{nihongo||{{linktext|草|薙|の|剣}}|'''Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi'''}} is a legendary Japanese [[sword]] and one of three [[Imperial Regalia of Japan]]. It was originally called {{nihongo3|"Heavenly Sword of Gathering Clouds"|{{linktext|天|叢|雲|剣}}|'''Ame-no-Murakumo-no-Tsurugi'''}}, but its name was later changed to the more popular {{transl|ja|Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi}} ("Grass-Cutting Sword"). In folklore, the sword represents the [[virtue]] of [[Courage|valor]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last=Perez|first=Louis G.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=RHXG0JV9zEkC&pg=PA191|title=Japan at War: An Encyclopedia|date=2013|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-59884-741-3|pages=191–192|language=en}}</ref>
==Legends== [[File:YamataNoOrochi.jpg|thumb|Susanoo slays the eight-headed beast Yamata-no-Orochi]] [[File:Yamato-Takeru-with-Sword-Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi-by-Ogata-Gekko.png|thumb|Prince [[Yamato Takeru]] and his sword Kusanagi no Tsurugi]] The history of the {{transl|ja|Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi}} extends into legend. According to {{transl|ja|[[Kojiki]]}}, the god [[Susanoo]] encountered a grieving family of {{transl|ja|kunitsukami}} ("gods of the land") headed by {{nihongo|Ashinazuchi|足名椎}} in [[Izumo Province]]. When Susanoo inquired of Ashinazuchi, he told him that his family was being terrorized by the fearsome [[Yamata no Orochi]], an eight-headed serpent of [[Koshi Province (Japan)|Koshi]], who had consumed seven of the family's eight daughters, and that the creature was coming for his final daughter, {{nihongo|[[Kushinada-hime]]|奇稲田姫}}. Susanoo investigated the creature, and after an abortive encounter he returned with a plan to defeat it. In return, he asked for Kushinada-hime's hand in marriage, which was agreed. Transforming her temporarily into a comb (one interpreter reads this section as "using a comb he turns into [masquerades as] Kushinada-hime") to have her company during battle, he detailed his plan into steps.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&pg=PA110|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|pages=110|language=en}}</ref>
He instructed that eight vats of {{transl|ja|[[sake]]}} (rice wine) be prepared and put on individual platforms positioned behind a fence with eight gates. The monster took the bait and put one of its heads through each gate. With this distraction, Susanoo attacked and slew the beast (with his sword {{transl|ja|[[Worochi no Ara-masa]]}}),<ref>{{cite book |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |translator-link=William George Aston |translator-first=William George |translator-last=Aston |at=Book I, part 1, page 56 |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |date=2005 |isbn=9780804836746}}</ref> chopping off each head and then proceeded to do the same to the tails.<ref name=":1" /> In the fourth tail, he discovered a great sword inside the body of the serpent which he called {{transl|ja|Ame-no-Murakumo-no-Tsurugi}}. He presented the sword to the goddess [[Amaterasu]] to settle an old grievance.<ref name=":0" /> The [[Nihon Shoki]] adds more to the story. It says Susanoo had [[Ame-no-Fuyukinu]] deliver the sword.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web |date=2023-11-14 |title=Amenofuyukinu, Amenofukine {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9436 |access-date=2023-11-14 |archive-date=2023-11-14 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231114035349/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9436 |url-status=bot: unknown }}</ref> A rite at [[Hinomisaki Shrine]] honors this delivery to this day.<ref name=":2" />
[[File:Atsuta Shrine.jpg|thumb|[[Atsuta Shrine]] in [[Nagoya]] dates back to 100 CE during the reign of [[Emperor Keikō]] and houses the {{transl|ja|Kusanagi}} sword.]]
Generations later, during the reign of the 12th Emperor [[Emperor Keikō|Keikō]], {{transl|ja|Ame-no-Murakumo-no-Tsurugi}} was given to the great warrior, [[Yamato Takeru]], as part of a pair of gifts given by his aunt, [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]], the Shrine Maiden of [[Grand Shrine of Ise|Ise Shrine]], to protect her nephew in times of peril.<ref name=":0" />
These gifts came in handy when Yamato Takeru was lured onto an open grassland during a hunting expedition by a treacherous warlord. The lord had fiery arrows loosed to ignite the grass and trap Yamato Takeru in the field so that he would burn to death. He also killed the warrior's horse to prevent his escape. Desperately, Yamato Takeru used the {{transl|ja|Ame-no-Murakumo-no-Tsurugi}} to cut back the grass and remove fuel from the fire, but in doing so, he discovered that the sword enabled him to control the wind and cause it to move in the direction of his swing. Taking advantage of this magic, Yamato Takeru used his other gift, fire strikers, to enlarge the fire in the direction of the lord and his men, and he used the winds controlled by the sword to sweep the blaze toward them. In triumph, Yamato Takeru renamed the sword {{transl|ja|Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi}} ("Grass-Cutting Sword") to commemorate his narrow escape and victory. Eventually, Yamato Takeru married and later fell in battle against a monster, after ignoring his wife's advice to take the sword with him.<ref name=":0" />
==Folklore== Although the sword is mentioned in the {{transl|ja|[[Kojiki]]}}, this book is a collection of Japanese myths and is not considered a historical document. The first reliable historical mention of the sword is in the {{transl|ja|[[Nihon Shoki]]}}.<ref name="Nihongi">{{cite book |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |translator-link=William George Aston |translator-first=William George |translator-last=Aston |at=Book I, part 1, page 53 |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |date=2005 |isbn=9780804836746}}</ref> Although the {{transl|ja|Nihon Shoki}} also contains mythological stories that are not considered reliable history, it records some events that were contemporary or nearly contemporary to its writing, and these sections of the book are considered historical. In the {{transl|ja|Nihon Shoki}}, the Kusanagi was removed from the Imperial palace in 688, and moved to [[Atsuta Shrine]] after the sword was blamed for causing [[Emperor Tenmu]] to fall ill. Along with the [[Magatama#Yasakani no Magatama|jewel]] ({{transl|ja|Yasakani no Magatama}}) and the [[Yata no Kagami|mirror]] ({{transl|ja|Yata no Kagami}}), it is one of the three [[Imperial Regalia of Japan]], the sword representing the virtue of valor.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&pg=PA115|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|pages=115|language=en}}</ref>
[[File:三種の神器 1200x1200.png|thumb|Artist's impressions of the (unseen) Imperial Regalia of Japan]]
{{transl|ja|Kusanagi}} is allegedly kept at Atsuta Shrine but is not available for public display.<ref name=":0" /> During the [[Edo period]], while performing various repairs and upkeep at Atsuta Shrine, including replacement of the outer wooden box housing the sword, the Shinto priest Matsuoka Masanao claimed to have been one of several priests to have seen the sword. Per his account, "a stone box was inside a wooden box of length {{convert|150|cm|abbr=on}}, with red clay stuffed into the gap between them. Inside the stone box was a hollowed log of a camphor tree, acting as another box, with an interior lined with gold. Above that was placed a sword. Red clay was also stuffed between the stone box and the camphor tree box. The sword was about {{convert|82|cm|abbr=on}} long. Its blade resembled a [[Sweet flag|calamus]] leaf. The middle of the sword had a thickness from the grip about {{convert|18|cm|abbr=on}} with an appearance like a fish spine. The sword was fashioned in a white metallic color, and well maintained." After witnessing the sword, the grand priest was banished and the other priests, except for Matsuoka, died from strange diseases. The above account therefore comes from the only survivor, Matsuoka.<ref>First appears in {{Nihongo|Endorsement of Gyokusensyu|玉籤集裏書|Gyokusenshū uragaki}} from around 1725, written by [[:ja:玉木正英|Tamaki Masahide]], a Shinto priest of [[Umenomiya Shrine]]. This text is then mentioned in the {{Nihongo|Investigation of Imperial Regalia|神器考證|Jingi Kōshō}}, published 1898, written by [[:ja:栗田寛|Kurita Hiroshi]]. Kokugakuin. http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/815487</ref>
In ''[[The Tale of the Heike]]'', a collection of oral stories transcribed in 1371, the sword is lost at sea after the defeat of the [[Taira clan|Heike]] in the [[Battle of Dan-no-ura]], a naval battle that ended in the defeat of the Heike clan forces and the child [[Emperor Antoku]] at the hands of [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]]. In the tale, upon hearing of the Navy's defeat, the Emperor's grandmother, [[Taira no Tokiko]], led the Emperor and his entourage to commit suicide by drowning in the waters of the strait, taking with her two of the three Imperial Regalia: the sacred jewel and the sword Kusanagi.<ref name="Tales 2006">{{cite book |title=The Tales of the Heike |url=https://archive.org/details/talesheiketransl00wats |url-access=limited |page=[https://archive.org/details/talesheiketransl00wats/page/n154 142] |publisher=Columbia University Press |date=2006 |isbn=9780231138024 |translator-last1=Watson |translator-first1=Burton}}</ref> The sacred mirror was recovered in extremis when one of the ladies-in-waiting was about to jump with it into the sea.<ref name="Tales 1988">{{cite book |title=The Tale of the Heike |translator-last=McCullough |translator-first=Helen Craig |publisher=Stanford University Press |date=1988 |isbn=9780804714181}}</ref> Although the sacred jewel is said to have been found in its casket floating on the waves, {{transl|ja|Kusanagi}} was lost forever. Although written about historical events, ''[[The Tale of the Heike]]'' is a collection of epic poetry passed down orally and written down nearly 200 years after the actual events, so it has questionable reliability as a historical document.
Another story holds that the sword was reportedly stolen again in the sixth century by a monk from [[Silla]].<ref name="Tales 1988" /> However, his ship allegedly sank at sea, allowing the sword to wash ashore at Ise, where it was recovered by Shinto priests.
==Current status== Because no one is allowed to see the sword due to its divinity and Shinto tradition, the exact shape and condition of the sword has not been confirmed. The most recent appearance of the sword was in 2019 when Emperor [[Naruhito]] [[2019 Japanese imperial transition|ascended the throne]]; the sword (as well as the jewel [[Yasakani no Magatama]], the Emperor's [[Privy Seal of Japan|privy seal]] and the [[State Seal of Japan|State Seal]]) were shrouded in packages.
Replicas of the sword were made as early as the 9th century, and the original is entrusted to [[Atsuta Shrine]] in [[Nagoya]].<ref name="encyclopedia-imp">{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Encyclopedia of Japan | title = Kurayoshi Plain | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-04-12 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo}}</ref> According to Shinsuke Takenaka of the Institute of Moralogy, a 12th-century replica preserved in the Imperial palace is the one used in coronation ceremonies,<ref name="bbctres">{{cite web |title=Akihito and Japan's Imperial Treasures that make a man an emperor |website=BBC |author=Anna Jones |date=27 April 2019 |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/world-asia-47931671 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220324032626/https://www.bbc.com/news/world-asia-47931671 |archive-date=March 24, 2022}}</ref> probably due to the fragility of the original sword.
==Other emperors' swords== The {{transl|ja|Kusanagi}} sword is always hidden because of its divinity, and it is put in a box and put up by the chamberlain at the time of the [[Enthronement of the Japanese emperor|enthronement ceremony]]. However, the Japanese sword held up by the emperor's chamberlain, which can be seen at various imperial ceremonies, is always close to the emperor as an amulet, and is called {{nihongo3|meaning 'sword of the throne in the daytime'|昼御座御剣|Hi no Omashi no Gyoken}}. {{transl|ja|Hi no Omashi no Gyoken}} has changed over time; at present, two {{transl|ja|[[tachi]]}} made by swordsmiths Nagamitsu and Yukihira in the [[Kamakura period]] play the role.<ref name="hachi191108">[https://twitter.com/EeoduLzbYVjTprk/status/1192601246122643456 儀式で天皇陛下のお側に登場する太刀はもちろん神器ではなく、平安以来の天皇の守り刀「昼御座御剣（ひのおましのごけん）」]. Tadamoto Hachijō. November 8, 2019</ref> Apart from these swords, the Imperial Family owns many swords, which are managed by the Imperial Household Agency. For example, one of the {{transl|ja|[[Tenka-Goken]]}}, {{transl|ja|Onimaru}} is owned by the Imperial Family.
==The sword of the Crown Prince of Japan== The Japanese crown prince has inherited two {{transl|ja|[[tachi]]}}, {{transl|ja|Tsubokiri no Gyoken}} or {{nihongo3|meaning "sword that cut a pot"|[[:ja:壺切御剣|壺切御剣]]|Tsubokiri no Mitsurugi}}, and {{nihongo3|meaning "sword made by Yukihira"|行平御剣|Yukihira Gyoken}}. While the {{transl|ja|Kusanagi}} sword is forbidden to be seen because of its divinity and is always kept in a box, the Crown Prince's sword is worn by the Crown Prince with the traditional costume {{transl|ja|[[sokutai]]}} at an official ceremony of the Imperial Household.<ref name="mai201107">{{Cite web|url=https://mainichi.jp/articles/20201107/k00/00m/040/036000c|title=秋篠宮さまに授けられる皇太子の守り刀 ベールに包まれた「壺切御剣」とは?|date=November 7, 2020|access-date=November 7, 2020|publisher=[[Mainichi Shimbun]]|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201107040543/https://mainichi.jp/articles/20201107/k00/00m/040/036000c|archive-date=2020-11-07}}</ref>
The {{transl|ja|Tsubokiri}} sword is the most important sword owned by the Crown Prince, given by the Emperor as proof of the official Crown Prince after the ceremony of his inauguration. Its origin is that it was given by [[Emperor Uda]] when [[Emperor Daigo]] became Crown Prince in 893, and the present, the {{transl|ja|Tsubokiri}} sword is the second generation, made in the late [[Heian period]]. The {{transl|ja|Yukihira}} sword is a {{transl|ja|tachi}} made by Yukihira, a swordsmith in the [[Kamakura period]], and the Crown Prince inherits it from the Emperor before his inauguration ceremony and wears it in various Imperial events except for the {{transl|ja|[[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niiname-sai]]}} festival.<ref name="mai201107"/> This Yukihira sword is different from the Emperor's {{transl|ja|Hi no Omashi no Gyoken}}.<ref name="hachi191108"/>
==See also== * [[Japanese sword]] * [[Ame-no-ohabari]]
==References== {{reflist|30em}} *{{Cite web |url=http://www.freidok.uni-freiburg.de/volltexte/4635/pdf/Naumann_The_kusanagi_sword.pdf |title=The kusanagi sword |first=Nelly |last=Naumann |date=1992 |author-link=Nelly Naumann }} In ''Nenrin-Jahresringe: Festgabe für Hans A. Dettmer.'' Ed. Klaus Müller. Wiesbaden: Harrassowitz, 1992. [158]–170.

revision.text:
{{Redirect|Kusanagi|3=Kusanagi (disambiguation)}} {{short description|Legendary sword of the Imperial Regalia of Japan}} {{italic title|reason=[[Japanese words and phrases]]}} {{Multiple issues| {{refimprove|date=November 2011}} {{more footnotes|date=November 2011}} }} {{jmyth navbox tall}} {{nihongo||{{linktext|草|薙|の|剣}}|'''Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi'''}} is a legendary Japanese [[sword]] and one of three [[Imperial Regalia of Japan]]. It was originally called {{nihongo3|"Heavenly Sword of Gathering Clouds"|{{linktext|天|叢|雲|剣}}|'''Ame-no-Murakumo-no-Tsurugi'''}}, but its name was later changed to the more popular {{transl|ja|Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi}} ("Grass-Cutting Sword"). In folklore, the sword represents the [[virtue]] of [[Courage|valor]].<ref name=":0">{{Cite book|last=Perez|first=Louis G.|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=RHXG0JV9zEkC&pg=PA191|title=Japan at War: An Encyclopedia|date=2013|publisher=ABC-CLIO|isbn=978-1-59884-741-3|pages=191–192|language=en}}</ref>
==Legends== [[File:YamataNoOrochi.jpg|thumb|Susanoo slays the eight-headed beast Yamata-no-Orochi]] [[File:Yamato-Takeru-with-Sword-Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi-by-Ogata-Gekko.png|thumb|Prince [[Yamato Takeru]] and his sword Kusanagi no Tsurugi]] The history of the {{transl|ja|Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi}} extends into legend. According to {{transl|ja|[[Kojiki]]}}, the god [[Susanoo]] encountered a grieving family of {{transl|ja|kunitsukami}} ("gods of the land") headed by {{nihongo|Ashinazuchi|足名椎}} in [[Izumo Province]]. When Susanoo inquired of Ashinazuchi, he told him that his family was being terrorized by the fearsome [[Yamata no Orochi]], an eight-headed serpent of [[Koshi Province (Japan)|Koshi]], who had consumed seven of the family's eight daughters, and that the creature was coming for his final daughter, {{nihongo|[[Kushinada-hime]]|奇稲田姫}}. Susanoo investigated the creature, and after an abortive encounter he returned with a plan to defeat it. In return, he asked for Kushinada-hime's hand in marriage, which was agreed. Transforming her temporarily into a comb (one interpreter reads this section as "using a comb he turns into [masquerades as] Kushinada-hime") to have her company during battle, he detailed his plan into steps.<ref name=":1">{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&pg=PA110|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|pages=110|language=en}}</ref>
He instructed that eight vats of {{transl|ja|[[sake]]}} (rice wine) be prepared and put on individual platforms positioned behind a fence with eight gates. The monster took the bait and put one of its heads through each gate. With this distraction, Susanoo attacked and slew the beast (with his sword {{transl|ja|[[Worochi no Ara-masa]]}}),<ref>{{cite book |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |translator-link=William George Aston |translator-first=William George |translator-last=Aston |at=Book I, part 1, page 56 |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |date=2005 |isbn=9780804836746}}</ref> chopping off each head and then proceeded to do the same to the tails.<ref name=":1" /> In the fourth tail, he discovered a great sword inside the body of the serpent which he called {{transl|ja|Ame-no-Murakumo-no-Tsurugi}}. He presented the sword to the goddess [[Amaterasu]] to settle an old grievance.<ref name=":0" /> The [[Nihon Shoki]] adds more to the story. It says Susanoo had [[Ame-no-Fuyukinu]] deliver the sword.<ref name=":2">{{Cite web |date=2023-11-14 |title=Amenofuyukinu, Amenofukine {{!}} 國學院大學デジタルミュージアム |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9436 |access-date=2023-11-14 |archive-date=2023-11-14 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20231114035349/https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/id=9436 |url-status=bot: unknown }}</ref> A rite at [[Hinomisaki Shrine]] honors this delivery to this day.<ref name=":2" />
[[File:Atsuta Shrine.jpg|thumb|[[Atsuta Shrine]] in [[Nagoya]] dates back to 100 CE during the reign of [[Emperor Keikō]] and houses the {{transl|ja|Kusanagi}} sword.]]
Generations later, during the reign of the 12th Emperor [[Emperor Keikō|Keikō]], {{transl|ja|Ame-no-Murakumo-no-Tsurugi}} was given to the great warrior, [[Yamato Takeru]], as part of a pair of gifts given by his aunt, [[Yamatohime-no-mikoto]], the Shrine Maiden of [[Grand Shrine of Ise|Ise Shrine]], to protect her nephew in times of peril.<ref name=":0" />
These gifts came in handy when Yamato Takeru was lured onto an open grassland during a hunting expedition by a treacherous warlord. The lord had fiery arrows loosed to ignite the grass and trap Yamato Takeru in the field so that he would burn to death. He also killed the warrior's horse to prevent his escape. Desperately, Yamato Takeru used the {{transl|ja|Ame-no-Murakumo-no-Tsurugi}} to cut back the grass and remove fuel from the fire, but in doing so, he discovered that the sword enabled him to control the wind and cause it to move in the direction of his swing. Taking advantage of this magic, Yamato Takeru used his other gift, fire strikers, to enlarge the fire in the direction of the lord and his men, and he used the winds controlled by the sword to sweep the blaze toward them. In triumph, Yamato Takeru renamed the sword {{transl|ja|Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi}} ("Grass-Cutting Sword") to commemorate his narrow escape and victory. Eventually, Yamato Takeru married and later fell in battle against a monster, after ignoring his wife's advice to take the sword with him.<ref name=":0" />
==Folklore== Although the sword is mentioned in the {{transl|ja|[[Kojiki]]}}, this book is a collection of Japanese myths and is not considered a historical document. The first reliable historical mention of the sword is in the {{transl|ja|[[Nihon Shoki]]}}.<ref name="Nihongi">{{cite book |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |translator-link=William George Aston |translator-first=William George |translator-last=Aston |at=Book I, part 1, page 53 |publisher=Tuttle Publishing |date=2005 |isbn=9780804836746}}</ref> Although the {{transl|ja|Nihon Shoki}} also contains mythological stories that are not considered reliable history, it records some events that were contemporary or nearly contemporary to its writing, and these sections of the book are considered historical. In the {{transl|ja|Nihon Shoki}}, the Kusanagi was removed from the Imperial palace in 688, and moved to [[Atsuta Shrine]] after the sword was blamed for causing [[Emperor Tenmu]] to fall ill. Along with the [[Magatama#Yasakani no Magatama|jewel]] ({{transl|ja|Yasakani no Magatama}}) and the [[Yata no Kagami|mirror]] ({{transl|ja|Yata no Kagami}}), it is one of the three [[Imperial Regalia of Japan]], the sword representing the virtue of valor.<ref>{{Cite book|last=Roberts|first=Jeremy|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=xdfgjV2kw6oC&pg=PA115|title=Japanese Mythology A to Z|date=2009|publisher=Infobase Publishing|isbn=978-1-4381-2802-3|pages=115|language=en}}</ref>
[[File:三種の神器 1200x1200.png|thumb|Artist's impressions of the (unseen) Imperial Regalia of Japan]]
{{transl|ja|Kusanagi}} is allegedly kept at Atsuta Shrine but is not available for public display.<ref name=":0" /> During the [[Edo period]], while performing various repairs and upkeep at Atsuta Shrine, including replacement of the outer wooden box housing the sword, the Shinto priest Matsuoka Masanao claimed to have been one of several priests to have seen the sword. Per his account, "a stone box was inside a wooden box of length {{convert|150|cm|abbr=on}}, with red clay stuffed into the gap between them. Inside the stone box was a hollowed log of a camphor tree, acting as another box, with an interior lined with gold. Above that was placed a sword. Red clay was also stuffed between the stone box and the camphor tree box. The sword was about {{convert|82|cm|abbr=on}} long. Its blade resembled a [[Sweet flag|calamus]] leaf. The middle of the sword had a thickness from the grip about {{convert|18|cm|abbr=on}} with an appearance like a fish spine. The sword was fashioned in a white metallic color, and well maintained." After witnessing the sword, the grand priest was banished and the other priests, except for Matsuoka, died from strange diseases. The above account therefore comes from the only survivor, Matsuoka.<ref>First appears in {{Nihongo|Endorsement of Gyokusensyu|玉籤集裏書|Gyokusenshū uragaki}} from around 1725, written by [[:ja:玉木正英|Tamaki Masahide]], a Shinto priest of [[Umenomiya Shrine]]. This text is then mentioned in the {{Nihongo|Investigation of Imperial Regalia|神器考證|Jingi Kōshō}}, published 1898, written by [[:ja:栗田寛|Kurita Hiroshi]]. Kokugakuin. http://kindai.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/815487</ref>
In ''[[The Tale of the Heike]]'', a collection of oral stories transcribed in 1371, the sword is lost at sea after the defeat of the [[Taira clan|Heike]] in the [[Battle of Dan-no-ura]], a naval battle that ended in the defeat of the Heike clan forces and the child [[Emperor Antoku]] at the hands of [[Minamoto no Yoshitsune]]. In the tale, upon hearing of the Navy's defeat, the Emperor's grandmother, [[Taira no Tokiko]], led the Emperor and his entourage to commit suicide by drowning in the waters of the strait, taking with her two of the three Imperial Regalia: the sacred jewel and the sword Kusanagi.<ref name="Tales 2006">{{cite book |title=The Tales of the Heike |url=https://archive.org/details/talesheiketransl00wats |url-access=limited |page=[https://archive.org/details/talesheiketransl00wats/page/n154 142] |publisher=Columbia University Press |date=2006 |isbn=9780231138024 |translator-last1=Watson |translator-first1=Burton}}</ref> The sacred mirror was recovered in extremis when one of the ladies-in-waiting was about to jump with it into the sea.<ref name="Tales 1988">{{cite book |title=The Tale of the Heike |translator-last=McCullough |translator-first=Helen Craig |publisher=Stanford University Press |date=1988 |isbn=9780804714181}}</ref> Although the sacred jewel is said to have been found in its casket floating on the waves, {{transl|ja|Kusanagi}} was lost forever. Although written about historical events, ''[[The Tale of the Heike]]'' is a collection of epic poetry passed down orally and written down nearly 200 years after the actual events, so it has questionable reliability as a historical document.
Another story holds that the sword was reportedly stolen again in the sixth century by a monk from [[Silla]].<ref name="Tales 1988" /> However, his ship allegedly sank at sea, allowing the sword to wash ashore at Ise, where it was recovered by Shinto priests.
==Current status== Because no one is allowed to see the sword due to its divinity and Shinto tradition, the exact shape and condition of the sword has not been confirmed. The most recent appearance of the sword was in 2019 when Emperor [[Naruhito]] [[2019 Japanese imperial transition|ascended the throne]]; the sword (as well as the jewel [[Yasakani no Magatama]], the Emperor's [[Privy Seal of Japan|privy seal]] and the [[State Seal of Japan|State Seal]]) were shrouded in packages.
Replicas of the sword were made as early as the 9th century, and the original is entrusted to [[Atsuta Shrine]] in [[Nagoya]].<ref name="encyclopedia-imp">{{cite encyclopedia | encyclopedia = Encyclopedia of Japan | title = Kurayoshi Plain | url = http://rekishi.jkn21.com/ | access-date = 2012-04-12 | year = 2012 | publisher = Shogakukan | location = Tokyo}}</ref> According to Shinsuke Takenaka of the Institute of Moralogy, a 12th-century replica preserved in the Imperial palace is the one used in coronation ceremonies,<ref name="bbctres">{{cite web |title=Akihito and Japan's Imperial Treasures that make a man an emperor |website=BBC |author=Anna Jones |date=27 April 2019 |url=https://www.bbc.com/news/world-asia-47931671 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220324032626/https://www.bbc.com/news/world-asia-47931671 |archive-date=March 24, 2022}}</ref> probably due to the fragility of the original sword.
==Other emperors' swords== The {{transl|ja|Kusanagi}} sword is always hidden because of its divinity, and it is put in a box and put up by the chamberlain at the time of the [[Enthronement of the Japanese emperor|enthronement ceremony]]. However, the Japanese sword held up by the emperor's chamberlain, which can be seen at various imperial ceremonies, is always close to the emperor as an amulet, and is called {{nihongo3|meaning 'sword of the throne in the daytime'|昼御座御剣|Hi no Omashi no Gyoken}}. {{transl|ja|Hi no Omashi no Gyoken}} has changed over time; at present, two {{transl|ja|[[tachi]]}} made by swordsmiths Nagamitsu and Yukihira in the [[Kamakura period]] play the role.<ref name="hachi191108">[https://twitter.com/EeoduLzbYVjTprk/status/1192601246122643456 儀式で天皇陛下のお側に登場する太刀はもちろん神器ではなく、平安以来の天皇の守り刀「昼御座御剣（ひのおましのごけん）」]. Tadamoto Hachijō. November 8, 2019</ref> Apart from these swords, the Imperial Family owns many swords, which are managed by the Imperial Household Agency. For example, one of the {{transl|ja|[[Tenka-Goken]]}}, {{transl|ja|Onimaru}} is owned by the Imperial Family.
==The sword of the Crown Prince of Japan== The Japanese crown prince has inherited two {{transl|ja|[[tachi]]}}, {{transl|ja|Tsubokiri no Gyoken}} or {{nihongo3|meaning "sword that cut a pot"|[[:ja:壺切御剣|壺切御剣]]|Tsubokiri no Mitsurugi}}, and {{nihongo3|meaning "sword made by Yukihira"|行平御剣|Yukihira Gyoken}}. While the {{transl|ja|Kusanagi}} sword is forbidden to be seen because of its divinity and is always kept in a box, the Crown Prince's sword is worn by the Crown Prince with the traditional costume {{transl|ja|[[sokutai]]}} at an official ceremony of the Imperial Household.<ref name="mai201107">{{Cite web|url=https://mainichi.jp/articles/20201107/k00/00m/040/036000c|title=秋篠宮さまに授けられる皇太子の守り刀 ベールに包まれた「壺切御剣」とは?|date=November 7, 2020|access-date=November 7, 2020|publisher=[[Mainichi Shimbun]]|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20201107040543/https://mainichi.jp/articles/20201107/k00/00m/040/036000c|archive-date=2020-11-07}}</ref>
The {{transl|ja|Tsubokiri}} sword is the most important sword owned by the Crown Prince, given by the Emperor as proof of the official Crown Prince after the ceremony of his inauguration. Its origin is that it was given by [[Emperor Uda]] when [[Emperor Daigo]] became Crown Prince in 893, and the present, the {{transl|ja|Tsubokiri}} sword is the second generation, made in the late [[Heian period]]. The {{transl|ja|Yukihira}} sword is a {{transl|ja|tachi}} made by Yukihira, a swordsmith in the [[Kamakura period]], and the Crown Prince inherits it from the Emperor before his inauguration ceremony and wears it in various Imperial events except for the {{transl|ja|[[Niiname-no-Matsuri|Niiname-sai]]}} festival.<ref name="mai201107"/> This Yukihira sword is different from the Emperor's {{transl|ja|Hi no Omashi no Gyoken}}.<ref name="hachi191108"/>
==See also== * [[Japanese sword]] * [[Ame-no-ohabari]]
==References== {{reflist|30em}} *{{Cite web |url=http://www.freidok.uni-freiburg.de/volltexte/4635/pdf/Naumann_The_kusanagi_sword.pdf |title=The kusanagi sword |first=Nelly |last=Naumann |date=1992 |author-link=Nelly Naumann }} In ''Nenrin-Jahresringe: Festgabe für Hans A. Dettmer.'' Ed. Klaus Müller. Wiesbaden: Harrassowitz, 1992. [158]–170.
